toolbar.hint: Click any person to inspect · Yellow ring = You

outbreaks.label: COVID-19
outbreaks.desc: 10–20% long COVID; ME/CFS subset ~2–3% of infections. Most extensively studied post-viral syndrome in history.

outbreaks.label: SARS (2003)
outbreaks.desc: 27% ME/CFS at 4-year follow-up (Hong Kong cohort). Highest documented post-viral CFS rate for a respiratory virus.

outbreaks.label: EBV / Mono
outbreaks.desc: Dubbo study: 11% ME/CFS at 6 months regardless of pathogen. Primary trigger for classical ME/CFS pre-COVID.

outbreaks.label: H1N1 (2009)
outbreaks.desc: AHR 2.04 for persistent fatigue, strongest in adults under 30. First pandemic to generate large-scale post-viral longitudinal data.

outbreaks.label: Influenza (seasonal)
outbreaks.desc: AHR 1.51 for ME/CFS vs matched controls across 20-year population study. 41% post-infection fatigue in meta-analysis.

outbreaks.label: Q Fever
outbreaks.desc: Dubbo cohort: 11% ME/CFS at 6 months. Post-Q-Fever fatigue syndrome well-characterised; Coxiella burnetii.

outbreaks.label: Ross River Virus
outbreaks.desc: Dubbo cohort original trigger. ~30% persistent arthralgia and fatigue at 6 months.

outbreaks.label: Dengue Fever
outbreaks.desc: Up to 82% persistent fatigue reported. Severe post-dengue syndrome including cognitive impairment.

outbreaks.label: West Nile Virus
outbreaks.desc: 31% persistent fatigue at follow-up. Neurological sequelae including encephalitis overlap with ME/CFS phenotype.

outbreaks.label: Lyme Disease
outbreaks.desc: Post-Treatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS) in 10–20% of treated patients. Fatigue, cognitive difficulty, pain; significant ME/CFS overlap.

outbreaks.label: Ebola (post-infection)
outbreaks.desc: 9–28% persistent fatigue in survivors. Sequelae include arthralgia, uveitis, neurological effects. Post-Ebola syndrome formally recognised.

outbreaks.label: Mold / Mycotoxin
outbreaks.desc: Post-monsoon or flooding mold bloom. Mycotoxin-driven systemic inflammation mimics ME/CFS; cognitive impairment, fatigue, and immune dysregulation.

outbreaks.label: Wildfire / Retardant
outbreaks.desc: Fine particulate (PM2.5) plus fire retardant chemical exposure. Combined oxidative stress and immune disruption; long-term respiratory and neurological sequelae.

outbreaks.label: Chemical Spill
outbreaks.desc: Organophosphate or industrial chemical exposure. Gulf War Illness is the canonical post-chemical ME/CFS-like syndrome; documented in multiple industrial incidents.

outbreaks.label: Natural Disaster
outbreaks.desc: Stress-mediated immunosuppression plus displacement, contaminated water, secondary infections. Elevated post-disaster CFS incidence documented post-Katrina and similar events.

you_profile.heading: Your profile
you_profile.description: Set your character's baseline before the outbreak. These factors determine how your risk compares to others in the community.

fields.label: Neurodivergence
fields.note: ADHD / autism / dyslexia. Elevated ANS dysregulation and ME/CFS rates documented across multiple cohorts.

fields.label: Emotional stress load
fields.note: Ongoing emotional burden (distinct from accumulated trauma). Primes HPA axis reactivity.

fields.label: Mechanical brainstem load
fields.note: Upper cervical instability, Chiari malformation, craniocervical instability. Direct brainstem compression is an emerging ME/CFS co-factor (Henderson, Bolognese).

fields.label: Digestive health
fields.note: IBS, gut permeability, dysbiosis. ME/CFS co-morbidity: 50–80%. Gut-brain axis disruption is mechanistically implicated.

fields.label: Metabolic health
fields.note: Mitochondrial dysfunction, insulin resistance, thyroid conditions. Naviaux metabolic model implicates cellular energy metabolism.

fields.label: Physical fitness
fields.note: Baseline fitness and exercise capacity. Higher fitness supports immune resilience and post-infection recovery. Higher = better.

fields.label: Diet quality
fields.note: Nutrient density, microbiome support, inflammatory load. Higher = better quality. Poor diet amplifies immune dysregulation.

fields.label: Chronic stress / trauma
fields.note: Accumulated prior stress and trauma load. Distinct from current emotional stress — longer-term baseline. Wessely et al. 1995.

fields.label: Sleep quality
fields.note: Higher = better sleep. Poor sleep impairs immune clearance and recovery. Higher is protective.

fields.label: Immune strength
fields.note: Baseline immune competence. Higher = stronger immune function. Protective against post-viral conversion.

fields.label: Genetic susceptibility
fields.note: HLA gene associations, SNPs affecting immune regulation. Higher = greater inherited predisposition.

fields.label: Pre-existing conditions
fields.note: Autoimmune, psychiatric, or metabolic conditions prior to this infection.

fields.label: Social support
fields.note: Social support is protective. Higher = stronger support network. Buchwald et al. 2000.

fields.label: Mold / mycotoxin exposure
fields.note: Home or workplace mold. Mycotoxin-driven systemic inflammation and immune dysregulation.

fields.label: Chemical / toxin exposure
fields.note: Pesticides, solvents, occupational chemicals, fire retardants. Adds to oxidative and immune burden.

fields.label: Air quality (personal)
fields.note: PM2.5, ozone, and particulate load in your immediate environment. Worsens inflammatory and immune baseline.

fields.label: Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV)
fields.note: ~90% adult seropositivity. Primary classical ME/CFS trigger. Reactivation risk compounds with additional infections.

fields.label: HHV-6
fields.note: ~95% adult seropositivity. HHV-6 reactivation documented in ME/CFS; HHV-6B strain specifically implicated.

fields.label: Cytomegalovirus (CMV)
fields.note: ~60% adult seropositivity. Compounds cumulative herpesvirus immune burden.

fields.label: Lyme Disease (Borrelia)
fields.note: Post-Treatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS) has strong ME/CFS overlap. Prior Lyme significantly elevates post-viral syndrome risk.

fields.label: Mycoplasma
fields.note: Mycoplasma pneumoniae / fermentans. Nicolson et al.: elevated prevalence in ME/CFS. Adds to cumulative infection burden.

environment.footer_note: Transmission controls affect how the outbreak spreads. Societal stressors affect post-infection CFS risk independently.

controls.label: Social contact level
controls.note: 0 = isolated / distancing · 1 = dense / crowded. Scales effective contacts per person per day.

controls.label: Sanitation level
controls.note: 0 = poor (no handwashing, poor ventilation) · 1 = excellent. Reduces per-contact transmission probability.

detail.click_hint: Click any person in the simulation to see their individual profile, risk factors, and — if they develop ME/CFS — a brainstem disruption diagram.

generic.exposed: {name} was recently exposed. Symptoms may appear within days.
generic.infectious: {name} is in the acute phase and currently infectious.
generic.recovering: {name} is past the acute phase. Long-term outcome is still forming.
generic.long_covid: {name} has persistent post-viral symptoms. Many in this state recover over months; some do not.
generic.cfs: {name} has developed ME/CFS. Fatigue, PEM, and cognitive symptoms are limiting daily function.
generic.severe_cfs: {name} has severe ME/CFS. {name} is largely or fully bedbound.

viewpoint.healthy: You haven't been infected yet. Your risk factors are visible below — stay aware.
viewpoint.exposed: You've been exposed. You may not feel it yet, but the virus has entered your system.
viewpoint.infectious: You're in the acute phase. You're contagious. How your body handles this matters.
viewpoint.recovering: The acute phase has passed. How this resolves will be determined over the coming months — your autonomic involvement during acute illness is the key signal.
viewpoint.recovered: You recovered fully. Your risk factors were present, but the outcome went in your favour.
viewpoint.long_covid: You have persistent post-viral symptoms. You're in the recovery window — many people recover over 6–18 months. Some don't.
viewpoint.cfs: You've developed ME/CFS. This is your new reality. The brainstem disruption diagram below shows what's happening in your nervous system.
viewpoint.severe_cfs: You have severe ME/CFS. You are largely bedbound. The gap between your cognitive self and your physical capacity is profound.

sections.heading: What this simulates
sections.body: A viral or environmental outbreak moves through a community of 100 individuals. Each person's probability of developing ME/CFS post-infection is computed from a multi-factor risk model calibrated to published epidemiological research. Long COVID cases follow a decay curve toward recovery; ME/CFS cases represent the hard floor.

sections.heading: Post-viral illness is not new
sections.body: Post-viral conditions appear after every major pandemic. Historical review across 150 years shows large numbers affected after the 1890 Russian flu, the 1918 Spanish flu, SARS (2003), H1N1 (2009), and COVID-19. COVID-19 substantially accelerated research into underlying mechanisms.

sections.heading: Prevalence across pathogens
list: COVID-19: WHO 10–20% have symptoms beyond 3 months. Meta-analysis of 194 studies: 45% had at least one unresolved symptom at avg 126-day follow-up.
SARS (2003): 40% chronic fatigue at 4-year follow-up; 27% diagnosed ME/CFS (Hong Kong cohort).
Influenza: 41% fatigue prevalence in survivors (meta-analysis). 20-year study: AHR 1.51 for CFS vs controls.
Ebola: 9–28% persistent fatigue. West Nile: 31%. Dengue: up to 82%.
Dubbo studies (EBV, Q fever, Ross River): 11% ME/CFS at 6 months — pathogen-independent. Severity of acute infection was the main predictor.

sections.heading: The decay curve and the hard floor
sections.body: Long COVID prevalence in the U.S. declined from 18.9% (June 2022) to 11.0% (June 2023), then stabilised. Chicago mono study: 13% ME/CFS-like at 6 months → 7% at 12 months → 4% at 24 months. ~40–46% of remaining cases recover per 6-month window. The 4% floor represents those who do not recover. This simulation models both: Long COVID cases gradually recover; ME/CFS cases represent the persistent floor.

sections.heading: Key predictors (cross-pathogen)
list: Severity of acute illness — most replicated predictor across all studies.
Autonomic symptoms during acute phase — Chicago mono: strongest single predictor of ME/CFS conversion.
Female sex — 2–3× elevated risk consistently across pathogens.
Age 35–64 — peak risk for Long COVID phenotype (ONS); older adults elevated for influenza-related CFS.
Pre-existing conditions — autoimmune, metabolic, psychiatric history all elevate risk.
Social support, sleep quality, economic stress — modulating factors throughout recovery.

sections.heading: Environmental triggers
sections.body: Not all post-viral-syndrome triggers are viruses. Mycotoxin exposure (mold), organophosphate chemicals (Gulf War Illness), wildfire particulates, and post-disaster stress all generate ME/CFS-like syndromes through overlapping immune and autonomic dysregulation pathways. These are modelled as community-wide exposure events rather than person-to-person transmission.

sections.heading: Population-level burden
sections.body: Even a 2–5% chronic-illness conversion rate applied to pandemic-scale infections produces tens of millions added to the global disability burden — a slow-moving wave that follows the acute wave by 1–5 years and is much less visible in public discourse.

sections.heading: Brainstem disruption model
sections.body: For individuals who develop ME/CFS, the profile view shows a simplified brainstem/ANS diagram reflecting dorsal vagal complex hyperactivation (Porges), HPA axis blunting, and vagal dysregulation. Structures: Hypothalamus/HPA, NTS, DVC, Locus Coeruleus (LC), Vagus nerve.

sections.heading: References
sections.body: Wessely et al. 1995; Jason et al. 2006; Tomas & Newton 2018; Dubbo Infection Outcomes Study (Hickie et al.); Chicago mono cohort (Katz et al.); ONS Long COVID surveys 2022–2024; Hong Kong SARS follow-up (Lee et al.); Porges polyvagal theory; VanElzakker vagus nerve hypothesis; Naviaux metabolic model.

sections.heading: This is a model, not a prediction
sections.body: All values are illustrative. Individual outcomes are stochastic. The simulation is designed to make the systemic nature of post-viral illness tangible — not to provide clinical guidance.
